elements.sent-2411-001: Automatic Cobb angle measurement from X-ray images is crucial for scoliosis screening and diagnosis.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, most existing regression-based methods and segmentation-based methods struggle with inaccurate spine representations or mask connectivity/fragmentation issues.
elements.sent-2411-003: Besides, landmark-based methods suffer from insufficient training data and annotations.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework including Self-Generation pipeline and Low-Rank Approximation representation (SG-LRA) for automatic Cobb angle measurement.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, we propose a parameterized spine contour representation based on LRA, which enables eigen-spine decomposition and spine contour reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-006: We can directly obtain spine contour with only regressed LRA coefficients, which form a more accurate spine representation than rectangular boxes.
elements.sent-2411-007: Also, we combine LRA coefficient regression with anchor box classification to solve inaccurate predictions and mask connectivity issues.
elements.sent-2411-008: Moreover, we develop a data engine with automatic annotation and automatic selection in an iterative manner, which is trained on a private Spinal2023 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-009: With our data engine, we generate the largest scoliosis X-ray dataset named Spinal-AI2024 largely without privacy leaks.
elements.sent-2411-010: Extensive experiments on public AASCE2019, private Spinal2023, and generated Spinal-AI2024 datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art Cobb angle measurement performance.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our code and Spinal-AI2024 dataset are available at https://github.com/Ernestchenchen/SG-LRA and https://github.com/Ernestchenchen/Spinal-AI2024, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-012: Early detection and treatment of scoliosis can help reduce the need for surgical interventions \cite{prabhu2012automatic .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The Cobb angle~\cite{scholten1987analysis is one of the most widely used clinical measures for evaluating and diagnosing the severity of scoliosis.}
elements.sent-2411-014: It is defined as the angle between parallel lines of the top of the upper end vertebra (the most tilted vertebrae on the curve) and the bottom of the lower one~\cite{mcleod2022anesthesia .}
elements.sent-2411-015: There are three regional spinal deformities associated with different spinal segments: proximal thoracic (PT), main thoracic (MT), and thoracolumbar/lumbar (TL/L), as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{page1fig1 (c).}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.97\linewidth]{cobb_angle.eps} \caption{Illustration of Cobb angle measurement. There are three categories of scoliosis: proximal thoracic (PT) curve, main thoracic (MT) curve, and thoracolumbar/lumbar (TL/L) curve, as shown in (c). In (a), handwritten 23°, 31°, and 34° are manually measured by experts, angles in green are measured based on rectangular boxes (also in green), and angles in red are measured based on our proposed contour boxes composed by spinal landmarks (drawn in (b)). (b) and (c) show two types of evaluations: maximum angle of three spinal curves, three regional angles, respectively.} \label{page1fig1}
elements.sent-2411-016: Currently, manual Cobb angle measurement on radiographic images like X-ray images by experts is still the mainstream way in medical institutions.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, it is time-consuming and often has potential errors~\cite{vrtovec2009review,pruijs1994variation , since it can be affected by spinal selection and inter-observer variability, which can be observed from the distances between manual angles and angles by landmarks in Fig.~\ref{page1fig1 (a).}}
elements.sent-2411-018: It has been validated that manual annotation of the Cobb angle exhibits fluctuations ranging from 2.8 to 8 degrees~\cite{sun2022comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Therefore, developing a reliable automatic Cobb angle measurement method is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-020: Traditional automatic Cobb angle measurement methods rely on hand-crafted features~\cite{sun2017direct,wu2017automatic , limiting model performance and generalization.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In recent years, some regression-based methods use rectangular boxes as the spine representation~\cite{khanal2020automatic,targ2016resnet , which suffer from reduced accuracy when spinal blocks are wedge-shaped or irregular, as shown in Fig.~\ref{page1fig1 (a).}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Segmentation-based methods are sensitive to image quality~\cite{lin2020seg4reg,wu2023automated , tending to predict connected or damaged spinal masks for low-quality inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Landmark-based methods necessitate vast amounts of training data and annotations~\cite{xu2021graph,yi2020vertebra,shao2021jaa , which are costly and time-consuming to collect and label, and are exacerbated by data scarcity due to patient privacy concerns.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this paper, we distill the above limitations as three main issues, and tackle them from three aspects: (a) How to achieve more accurate spine representation? (b) How to generate sufficient scoliosis images while protecting patient privacy? (c) How to obtain accurate annotations at low costs? Parameterized Spine Representation.
elements.sent-2411-025: We propose a novel automatic Cobb angle measurement framework named SG-LRA.
elements.sent-2411-026: We combine regression-based and landmark-based techniques, by using a set of spinal landmarks to represent the bounding box of spinal segments.
elements.sent-2411-027: Inspired by low-rank approximation (LRA) parameterized contour representation in object detection~\cite{park2022eigencontours,su2024lranet , we use LRA to learn eginespines from spine contours of training data, and reconstruct spine contours via LRA coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-028: To suppress inaccurate predictions and mask connectivity, we integrate LRA coefficient regression with anchor box classification to obtain spinal segment contours, in which sparse and dense assignments are integrated to more precisely detect spinal segments.. Privacy-Preserving Scoliosis Generation.
elements.sent-2411-029: We explore the latent diffusion model (LDM)~\cite{rombach2022high for scoliosis image synthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Specifically, we train LDM on our private dataset Spinal2023, utilizing a diffusion process to map raw images from pixel to latent space.
elements.sent-2411-031: During inference, the trained LDM with text-conditioned prompts can generate X-ray images.
elements.sent-2411-032: To mitigate privacy leaks~\cite{carlini2023extracting , we employ data augmentation during LDM training, promote data balance, and introduce a privacy review~\cite{packhauser2023generation to exclude potentially privacy-compromising samples based on structural and pixel similarities, coupled with manual verification.. Cost-Effective Semi-Supervised Annotation.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Inspired from semi-supervised pseudo-labeling~\cite{rizve2021defense and multi-stage interactive annotation~\cite{kirillov2023segment , we propose a new data engine, which drives an iterative pipeline with automatic annotation and selection.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: It consists of four stages: pseudo-labeling, auto-annotation, manual-assisted annotation, and privacy review.
elements.sent-2411-035: At each iteration, spine contour detection network is first re-trained using current dataset, and its trained model is used for pseudo-labeling so as to further update the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-036: This iterative pipeline culminates in our open-source dataset named Spinal-AI2024, which is the largest scoliosis X-ray dataset.
elements.sent-2411-037: The main contributions of this paper are threefold:.
elements.sent-2411-038: We propose a novel low-rank approximation parameterized spine representation for automatic Cobb angle measurement, in which LRA coefficient regression and anchor box classification with sparse and dense assignments are combined to detect irregular contours of spinal segments.
elements.sent-2411-039: We propose a new data engine with semi-supervised labeling and privacy review in an iterative manner, which enables the generation of large-scale scoliosis X-ray images largely without privacy leaks.
elements.sent-2411-040: To our knowledge, our open-source Spinal-AI2024 is the largest scoliosis X-ray dataset.
elements.sent-2411-041: Extensive experiments on AASCE2019, Spinal2023, and Spinal-AI2024 datasets show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art automatic Cobb angle measurement works for both maximum angle and three regional angles.
elements.sent-2411-042: Earlier methods~\cite{sun2017direct,wu2017automatic,xue2017direct,wu2018automated adopt traditional machine learning to approximate the relationship between image features and Cobb angles.}
elements.sent-2411-043: However, these methods suffer from performance and generalization issues due to loss of high-resolution details through downsampling~\cite{yi2020vertebra or reliance on hand-crafted features.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Recently, deep learning based methods~\cite{shao2021explicit,shao2023facial have gained increasing attention.}

elements.sent-2411-045: Regression-based methods~\cite{khanal2020automatic,targ2016resnet,wu2018automated,wang2019accurate aim to learn a direct mapping between the spinal shape representation and the Cobb angle, without explicit geometric calculations.}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, these approaches have limited performance for wedge-shaped or irregular vertebrae due to rectangular shape assumption.
elements.sent-2411-047: Segmentation-based methods~\cite{lin2020seg4reg,pan2019evaluation,chen2022automating usually rely on U-Net~\cite{ronneberger2015u or its modifications~\cite{horng2019cobb,wu2023automated , by performing pixel-level classification and then combining pixels as spinal regions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: However, these methods are sensitive to image quality, and often require pre-processing to address noise, resolution, and contrast issues~\cite{pednekar2018image,chen2022automating .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Landmark-based methods~\cite{sun2019deep,xu2021graph,zhou2023vertebral,yi2020vertebra,guo2022cobb focus on localizing spinal landmarks, which often requires vast training data.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In this paper, we overcome the data scarcity issue by predicting landmarks through LRA coefficient regression, leveraging our proposed data engine with automatic annotation and selection.
elements.sent-2411-051: Deep learning relies heavily on numerous labeled training data \cite{kazeminia2020gans .}
elements.sent-2411-052: In the medical image field, there is often a scarcity of annotated data due to cumbersome, time-consuming, and expensive image acquisition and annotation \cite{tucker2020generating .}
elements.sent-2411-053: To alleviate this limitation, previous works use data perturbation \cite{setio2016pulmonary or data synthesis \cite{patki2016synthetic .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Generative models like generative adversarial networks (GANs) \cite{karras2017progressive,goodfellow2020generative have demonstrated the capability to produce realistic, high-resolution images \cite{goodfellow2020generative,mescheder2017numerics,karras2020analyzing,brock2018large .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Recent studies \cite{carlini2023extracting indicate that diffusion models \cite{rombach2022high possess higher generation capacity than GANs.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Packh{\"a user \etal~\cite {packhauser2023generation utilized LDM to generate chest X-ray images, and proposed a sampling strategy that safeguards the privacy of sensitive biometric information.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, sharing sensitive data under stringent privacy regulations remains a critical challenge in medical research \cite{lo2015sharing,esteva2019guide .}
elements.sent-2411-058: In this paper, we also leverage LDM to generate scoliosis X-ray images.
elements.sent-2411-059: Additionally, we propose a privacy-auditing module combined with pseudo-label training, utilizing a comprehensive privacy similarity mechanism to filter out images that may expose patient privacy.
elements.sent-2411-060: Semi-supervised labeling methods can be categorized into consistency regularization and pseudo-labeling approaches.
elements.sent-2411-061: Consistency regularization methods \cite{honari2018improving,moskvyak2021semi rely on modality-specific augmentation for regularization.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Pseudo-labeling methods \cite{li2021synthetic predict labels for unlabeled data and train the model in a supervised manner.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Shi \etal~\cite{shi2018transductive adopted local neighborhood density-based confidence scores to facilitate pseudo-labeling.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Inspired by noise correction \cite{yi2019probabilistic , Wang \etal~\cite{wang2020repetitive updated pseudo-labels by an optimization framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Xie \etal~\cite{xie2020self demonstrated the effectiveness of self-training in supervised classification tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-066: However, these methods struggle with flawed pseudo-labels.
elements.sent-2411-067: Recently, Rizve \etal~\cite{rizve2021defense selected high-quality pseudo-labels from noisy samples based on uncertainty.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Kirillov \etal~\cite{kirillov2023segment built a data engine to augment data through manual-assisted, semi-automatic, and fully automatic phases.}
elements.sent-2411-069: In this paper, we employ a similar semi-automatic annotation pipeline to expand datasets, in which pseudo-label selection and manual assistance are integrated for quality improvement.
elements.sent-2411-070: The architecture of our framework is illustrated in Fig.~\ref{page3fig3 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Given our private Spinal2023 dataset, we first train Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high to generate vast spinal X-ray images.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Specifically, the generation starts from the original pixel space, undergoing diffusion.
elements.sent-2411-073: The textual condition and pixel features are then fed into the latent space, where cross-attention modules produce the final output.
elements.sent-2411-074: Also, we use Spinal2023 to train an initial model of spinal contour detection network.
elements.sent-2411-075: This network adopts ResNet50 \cite{he2016deep as backbone, and uses feature pyramid network (FPN) \cite{lin2017feature to extract multi-scale features.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Then, it uses regression and classification branches to predict LRA coefficients and positive anchor boxes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-077: In both branches, sparse and dense assignments are combined to improve the detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-078: Finally, we select LRA coefficients corresponding to positive anchor boxes to reconstruct spinal segment contours.
elements.sent-2411-079: The data engine integrates a loop with a screening mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-080: The screening process encompasses pixel-level privacy checks, sample-level legality reviews, and segment-level quality assessments.
elements.sent-2411-081: At each iteration, spine contour detection network is fine-tuned using current data, and its trained model is further used for pseudo-labeling so as to update the data and annotations.
elements.sent-2411-082: To address inaccuracy of rectangular box based spine representation in prior works, we utilize contour box composed by spinal landmarks to represent spinal segments.
elements.sent-2411-083: Given that spinal segments generally exhibit similar structural properties and notable correlations among their contours, we develop a new parameterized spinal contour representation method based on such correlations from training data.
elements.sent-2411-084: In particular, the boundary of a spinal segment is composed of $N$ vertices, i.e. landmarks, which can be represented as a vector $\mathbf{a = [ x_1,y_1, \cdots , x_N , y_N ]\in \mathbb{R ^{2N\times 1}$ containing x and y coordinates.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Then, we construct a spinal contour matrix $\mathbf{A = [\mathbf{a _1,\mathbf{a _2,\cdots,\mathbf{a _L ] \in \mathbb{R ^{2N \times L}$ from training data containing $L$ spinal segment instances.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Subsequently, we employ singular value decomposition (SVD) to extract the underlying structural relationship among $\mathbf{A}$: \begin{equation}\mathbf{A = U\Sigma V}^T= \sigma_1 \mathbf{u}_1 \mathbf{v}_1^T + \cdots + \sigma_r \mathbf{u}_r \mathbf{v}_r^T, \label{A:decompose}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{U = [\mathbf{u _1,\mathbf{u _2,\cdots,\mathbf{u _{2N ]\in \mathbb{R ^{2N \times 2N}$and $\mathbf{V = [\mathbf{v _1,\mathbf{v _2,\cdots,\mathbf{v _L]\in \mathbb{R ^{L \times L}$ are orthogonal matrices.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}A = U\Sigma V^T= \sigma_1 u_1 v_1^T + \cdots + \sigma_r u_r v_r^T, \label{A:decompose}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-087: Each singular vector represents an orthogonal direction in the eigen-space.
elements.sent-2411-088: By retaining the first $M$ singular values and their corresponding vectors while truncating the remaining low-rank components in Eq.~\eqref{A:decompose , we can approximate $\mathbf{A}$ as \begin{equation}\mathbf{A}_M = \sigma_1 \mathbf{u}_1 \mathbf{v}_1^T + \cdots + \sigma_M \mathbf{u}_M \mathbf{v}_M^T=\mathbf{U}_M \mathbf{\Sigma}_M \mathbf{V}_M^T,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{A _M$ is the best rank-M approximation of $\mathbf{A}$, since it minimizes the Frobenius norm $\|\mathbf{A - \mathbf{A _M\|_F$ \cite{blum2020foundations .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Then, we define $\mathbf{C _M = \mathbf{\Sigma _M \mathbf{V _M^T=[ \mathbf{c _1 , \mathbf{c _2 , \cdots , \mathbf{c _L ] \in \mathbb{R ^{M \times L}$, such that $\mathbf{A _M$ can be represented as \begin{equation}\mathbf{A}_M = [ \mathbf{U}_M\mathbf{c}_1 , \cdots , \mathbf{U}_M\mathbf{c}_L]=[ \tilde{\mathbf{a}}_1 , \cdots , \tilde{\mathbf{a}}_L], \label{A:approximated}\end{equation} where $\tilde{\mathbf{a _i = \mathbf{U _M\mathbf{c _i = [ \mathbf{u _1 , \mathbf{u _2 , \cdots , \mathbf{u _M ] \mathbf{c _i$ is the approximation of $\mathbf{a _i$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: We refer to $\mathbf{u _1, \ldots, \mathbf{u _M$ as eigen-spines, since they are eigen-vectors of the matrix $\mathbf{A A^T}$ and can be viewed as principal spinal segment shapes.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: For any $2N$-dimensional spinal segment contour $\mathbf{a}$, we can project it onto the eigen-spine space: \begin{equation}\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{U}_M^T \mathbf{a},\end{equation} where $\mathbf{c}\in \mathbb{R ^{M\times 1}$ is the LRA coefficient vector.}
elements.sent-2411-092: As shown in Fig.~\ref{page3fig3 , our spinal contour detection network contains regression and classification branch, in which the overall loss is defined as \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \lambda_{reg} \mathcal{L}_{reg}+\lambda_{cls} \mathcal{L}_{cls}, \label{eq:lra_total}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{L _{reg}$ and $\mathcal{L _{cls}$ are the losses for the regression and classification branches, respectively, and $\lambda_{reg}$ and $\lambda_{cls}$ control their importances.}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: After combining the outputs of regression and classification branches, we can obtain a LRA coefficient matrix $\mathbf{C _K\in \mathbb{R ^{M\times K}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The predicted spinal segment locations are calculated as \begin{equation}\mathbf{A}_K = \mathbf{U}_M \mathbf{C}_K,\end{equation} where $K$ denotes the number of detected spinal segments.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{The overall procedure of our data engine.} \label{alg:procedure} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Labeled dataset $D_{spinal2023}$, unlabeled dataset $D_{selected}$ generated by Stable Diffusion, sample selection index set $V_{selected}$ initialized as empty set, and spinal contour detection network with parameters $\Theta$. \ENSURE Labeled dataset $D_{SpinalAI2024}$. \STATE Train spinal contour detection network on $D_{spinal2023}$ and obtain initial parameters $\Theta^0$. Set $i \gets 1$. \label{alg:procedure:1} \WHILE{$V_{selected}$ is not converged} \STATE Pseudo-label samples in $D_{selected}$ using $\Theta^{i-1}$ with $\tau_c$-based positive spinal segment instance selection. \STATE Update $V_{selected}$ by auto-annotation, manual-assisted annotation, and privacy review. \STATE $D_{SpinalAI2024} \gets \{s^j|s^j\in D_{selected}, v^j=1 \}$. \STATE Fine-tune network from $\Theta^{i-1}$ using samples from $D_{spinal2023} \cup D_{SpinalAI2024}$, and obtain new parameters $\Theta^{i}$. Set $i \gets i+1$. \ENDWHILE \STATE Return $D_{SpinalAI2024}$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-095: Our data engine is composed of iteratively four stages: pseudo-labeling, auto-annotation, manual-assisted annotation, and privacy review.
elements.sent-2411-096: Before starting the data engine, we use Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2022high to generate an initial set of spinal X-ray images, as shown in Algorithm~\ref{alg:procedure .}}
elements.sent-2411-097: At the $i$-th iteration, to enable pseudo-labeling, we first train our spinal contour detection network on currently available labeled data composed of $D_{spinal2023}$ and $D_{SpinalAI2024}$, in which the trained model parameters are denoted as $\Theta^{i-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Then, we use $\Theta^{i-1}$ to label samples in $D_{selected}$.
elements.sent-2411-099: For the $j$-th sample $s^j$, the network predicts the locations of $K$ spinal segments $[ \mathbf{a _1^j , \mathbf{a _2^j , \cdots , \mathbf{a _K^j ]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: To filter inaccurate predictions, we use a confidence threshold $\tau_c$ to select positive spinal segment instances for $s^j$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{A}^j=\{\mathbf{a}_k^j |p^j_k \geq \tau_c \}, \label{eq:confidence}\end{equation} where $p^j_k$ denotes the probability/confidence of the $k$-th spinal segment output by spinal contour detection network.

elements.sent-2411-101: We perform segment-level and sample-level automatic selection.
elements.sent-2411-102: We remove spinal segment instances with area smaller than 200 pixel$^2$, remove instances with illegal landmark coordinates like negative values or positions beyond image boundaries, and remove instances whose landmark coordinates cannot form a valid contour.
elements.sent-2411-103: At the sample-level, we discard samples with fewer than 10 instances, in which the average number of spinal segments per image is 17 in real datasets.
elements.sent-2411-104: We also discard samples with spinal center distances larger than three times of the average distances.
elements.sent-2411-105: This sample-level selection can filter out samples with poor generation qualities.
elements.sent-2411-106: The index $v^j$ of sample $s^j$ is set to 1 in $V_{selected}$ if it is selected, and is set to 0 otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-107: This stage first adds annotations for samples with insufficient instances, and then manually corrects instances with inaccurate localization.
elements.sent-2411-108: We also remove non-realistic, spinal-fracture, and unclear samples.
elements.sent-2411-109: Finally, we remove generated samples with excessively high comprehensive similarity (CS) to real samples in $D_{spinal2023}$.
elements.sent-2411-110: The values of $V_{selected}$ are assigned to determine whether to discard certain samples: \begin{equation}v^j = \mathds{1}[\max_{r=1}^{N_r}\{CS(s^j,t^r)\} \leq \tau_{CS}],\end{equation} where $CS(\cdot)$ denotes CS function, $t^r$ denotes the $r$-th sample in $D_{spinal2023}$, $N_r$ is the number of samples in $D_{spinal2023}$, and $\tau_{CS}$ is a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-111: We compare $s^j$ with all real samples so that the privacy of each real sample is preserved via $\tau_{CS}$-based selection.
elements.sent-2411-112: To assess the degree of patient privacy leakage in spinal image generation, we introduce the definition of memorization~\cite{carlini2023extracting : a sample $s^j$ is considered $N_m$-completely memorized if it can be extracted from the model and there are at most $N_m$ training samples $t^r$ in the dataset such that $CS(s^j,t^r)\leq \tau_{CS}$.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Here, $N_m$ denotes the number of nearly identical samples to $s^j$, which can be close to zero by setting a strict $\tau_{CS}$ value.
elements.sent-2411-114: To measure the similarity in terms of luminance, contrast, and structural information between two images, we use structural similarity index measure (SSIM): \begin{equation}SSIM(s^j,t^r) \!=\! \frac{(2\mu_{s^j}\mu_{t^r} + c_1)(2\sigma_{s^jt^r} + c_2)}{(\mu_{s^j}^2 + \mu_{t^r}^2 + c_1)(\sigma_{s^j}^2 + \sigma_{t^r}^2 + c_2)}, \label{eq:ssim}\end{equation} where $\mu_{s^j}$ and $\sigma_{s^j ^2$ are the mean pixel value and the variance of $s^j$, respectively, $\sigma_{s^jt^r}$ is the covariance of $s^j$ and $t^r$, and $c_1$ and $c_2$ are constants to stabilize division by denominators.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Besides, we formulate the pixel similarity (PS) between gray-scale transformed $s^j$ and $t^r$ with sizes $H \times W$: \begin{equation}PS(s^j,t^r) \!=\! \frac{1}{HWI} \sum_{a=1}^{H} \sum_{b=1}^{W} |GS(s^j)_{ab} - GS(t^r)_{ab}|, \label{eq:PS}\end{equation} where $GS(\cdot)$ denotes gray-scale transformation, and $I$ denotes the number of gray scales (generally to be 255).
elements.sent-2411-116: By combing Eqs.~\eqref{eq:ssim and \eqref{eq:PS using trade-off parameters $\lambda_{ss}$ and $\lambda_{ps}$, we obtain the definition of CS: \begin{equation}CS(s^j,t^r)= \lambda_{ss} SSIM(s^j,t^r)+\lambda_{ps}PS(s^j,t^r). \label{eq:CS}\end{equation} After the above selection, we can obtain currently labeled generated dataset $D_{SpinalAI2024}$ using the selection index set $V_{selected}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: We then merge $D_{SpinalAI2024}$ with the existing $D_{spinal2023}$ to fine-tune the spinal contour detection network.
elements.sent-2411-118: We continue to generate pseudo-labels at the next iteration, and repeat this process until $V_{selected}$ converges.
elements.sent-2411-119: We evaluate Cobb angle measurement on public AASCE2019~\cite{wu2017automatic,wang2021evaluation benchmark, and our private Spinal2023 and open-source Spinal-AI2024 datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The dataset details are summarized in Table \ref{intro:datasets .}
elements.sent-2411-121: AASCE2019 contains 609 frontal spine X-ray images, in which each image is annotated by clinicians with 17 spinal segments and 68 landmarks, as well as PT, MT, and TL/L Cobb angles.
elements.sent-2411-122: This dataset exhibits a wide range of image intensities and Cobb angle variations.

elements.sent-2411-124: Spinal2023 is our private clinical dataset, which consists of 904 spine X-ray images.

elements.subsection-2411-011: Spinal Contour Detection via Latent Contour Representation
elements.sent-2411-250: For any $2N$-dimensional spinal segment contour $\mathbf{a}$, we can project it onto the eigen-spine space: \begin{equation}\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{U}_M^T \mathbf{a},\end{equation} where \highlight{the eigen-spine matrix $\mathbf{U _M \in \mathbb{R ^{2N \times M}$ projects $\mathbf{a}\in \mathbb{R ^{2N\times 1}$ into the latent space, and $\mathbf{c}\in \mathbb{R ^{M\times 1}$ is the latent contour coefficient vector.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-251: After combining the outputs of regression and classification branches, we can obtain a latent contour coefficient matrix $\mathbf{C _K\in \mathbb{R ^{M\times K}$.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{The overall procedure of our iterative data engine. } \label{alg:procedure} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Labeled dataset $D_{spinal2023}$, unlabeled dataset $D_{selected}$ generated by Stable Diffusion, sample selection index set $V_{selected}$ initialized as empty set, and spinal contour detection network with parameters $\Theta$. \ENSURE Labeled dataset $D_{spinalAI2024}$. \STATE Train spinal contour detection network on $D_{spinal2023}$, and obtain initial parameters $\Theta^0$. \STATE Set $i \gets 1$. \label{alg:procedure:2} \WHILE{$V_{selected}$ is not converged} \label{alg:procedure:3} \STATE Pseudo-label samples in $D_{selected}$ using $\Theta^{i-1}$ with $\tau_c$-based positive spinal segment instance selection. \STATE Update $V_{selected}$ by auto-annotation, manual-assisted annotation, and privacy review. \STATE $D_{spinalAI2024} \gets \{s^j|s^j\in D_{selected}, v^j=1 \}$. \STATE Fine-tune network from $\Theta^{i-1}$ using samples from $D_{spinal2023} \cup D_{spinalAI2024}$, and obtain new parameters $\Theta^{i}$. \STATE Set $i \gets i+1$. \ENDWHILE \label{alg:procedure:9} \STATE Return $D_{spinalAI2024}$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.subsection-2411-012: Iterative Data Engine with Image Self-Generation
elements.sent-2411-252: At the $i$-th iteration, to enable pseudo-labeling, we first train our spinal contour detection network on currently available labeled data composed of $D_{spinal2023}$ and $D_{spinalAI2024}$, in which the trained model parameters are denoted as $\Theta^{i-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-253: By combing Eqs.~\eqref{eq:ssim and \eqref{eq:PS using trade-off parameters $\lambda_{ss}$ and $\lambda_{ps}$, we obtain the definition of CS: \begin{equation}CS(s^j,t^r)= \lambda_{ss} SSIM(s^j,t^r)+\lambda_{ps}PS(s^j,t^r). \label{eq:CS}\end{equation} After the above selection, we can obtain currently labeled generated clean dataset $D_{spinalAI2024}$ using the selection index set $V_{selected}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-254: We then merge $D_{spinalAI2024}$ with the existing $D_{spinal2023}$ to fine-tune the spinal contour detection network.

elements.sent-2411-255: We evaluate Cobb angle estimation on public AASCE2019~\cite{wu2017automatic,wang2021evaluation benchmark, and our private Spinal2023 and open-source Spinal-AI2024 datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-256: Spinal-AI2024 is our generated dataset with totally 20,000 spine X-ray images, in which 16,000 and 4,000 images are used for training and testing, respectively.

elements.sent-2411-261: It can be seen that our CurvNet maintains top-tier performance on AASCE2019.
elements.sent-2411-262: Compared to regression-based methods like Faster-RCNN, CurvNet performs better, due to our latent contour parameterized spine representation for precise Cobb angle calculation.
elements.sent-2411-263: Compared to segmentation-based methods, CurvNet with sparse and dense assignments can more accurately detect spinal segment contours, better addressing issues of spinal mask connectivity and fragmentation.
elements.sent-2411-264: Compared to landmark-based methods, our latent contour coefficient regression combined with the anchor box classification branch can more accurately localize corner points, giving stronger robustness for non-rectangular spinal segments.

elements.sent-2411-266: Since the construction of Spinal-AI2024 involves our spinal contour detection network, the reported results of CurvNet on Spinal-AI2024 are obtained by re-training spinal contour detection network on Spinal-AI2024.
elements.sent-2411-267: It can be observed that our CurvNet significantly outperforms previous works in most cases.
elements.sent-2411-268: In particular, on all the three datasets, CurvNet performs the best on MT and TL/L Cobb angles, as well as maximum Cobb angle.
elements.sent-2411-269: This is mainly thanks to the combination of latent contour coefficient regression and anchor box classification.
elements.sent-2411-270: Note that the performance for PT cure is not as good, possibly because CurvNet focuses more on localizing irregular spinal segments rather than limiting the number of predicted spinal segments.
elements.sent-2411-271: We define a PT estimation error as ``large'' if the absolute difference between predicted and ground-truth angle exceeds $10^\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-272: There are 37 of 128 test images with large estimation errors:.
elements.sent-2411-273: To further validate this, we compute the average Cobb angle deviation when cervical landmarks are present in the predicted PT region: +14.3°, significantly larger than +3.1° when only thoracic landmarks (T1–T5) are detected.
elements.sent-2411-274: In contrast, our CurvNet consistently works well across small and large datasets.

elements.sent-2411-287: In our method, we adopt latent diffusion model over GAN due to the superior mode coverage and training stability.
elements.sent-2411-288: Additionally, diffusion model offers better privacy preservation by reducing memorization risk.
elements.sent-2411-289: To validate our image generation model, we train a StyleGAN2-ADA~\cite{karras2020training model on Spinal2023 for comparison, and compute the Fréchet inception distance (FID) score between real Spinal2023 images and generated images using a pre-trained ResNet-50.}
elements.sent-2411-290: The FID score of StyleGAN2-ADA is 41.3 with severe performance dropping in extreme cases like Cobb angle larger than 45°, which is significantly worse than 28.6 FID score of our diffusion based method.

elements.sent-2411-294: We have proposed a novel curvature angle estimation framework with latent contour representation based contour detection and iterative data engine based image self-generation.
elements.sent-2411-295: Latent contour coefficient regression and anchor box classification with sparse and dense assignments are combined to detect irregular contours of spinal segments.
elements.sent-2411-296: Besides, we have proposed an iterative data engine with semi-supervised labeling and privacy review, which culminates in the open-source largest scoliosis X-ray dataset Spinal-AI2024.
elements.sent-2411-297: Extensive experiments have demonstrated the effectiveness of our method, achieving state-of-the-art performance on public, private, and generated datasets for both maximum Cobb angle and three regional Cobb angles.
